stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \) with characteristic polynomial \( p(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - A) \). What is \( p([[lam]]) \)?
distractor_rationales: This adds \( bc \) instead of subtracting it (a sign error on the off-diagonal product).
This is \( \det(A) \), which equals \( p(0) \), not \( p([[lam]]) \) in general.
This drops the constant term \( \det(A) \) that appears when expanding \( (\lambda-a)(\lambda-d)-bc \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] & [[i]] \end{pmatrix} \) with characteristic polynomial \( p(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - A) \). What is \( p(0) \)?
distractor_rationales: This is \( \det(A) \) itself, forgetting the sign flip \( (-1)^n \) from negating a \(3\times3\) matrix.
This double-negates \( \det(A) \), landing back on \( \det(A) \) with the wrong sign relative to \( p(0) \).
This is the trace of \( A \), not \( p(0) \).
A characteristic polynomial evaluated at 0 need not vanish unless 0 is an eigenvalue.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: For \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a11]] & [[a12]] & [[a13]] \\ [[a21]] & [[a22]] & [[a23]] \\ [[a31]] & [[a32]] & [[a33]] \end{pmatrix} \), the characteristic polynomial \( p(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - A) = \lambda^3 - (\operatorname{tr} A)\lambda^2 + \cdots \). What is the coefficient of \( \lambda^2 \) in \( p(\lambda) \)?
solution: The \( \lambda^2 \) coefficient of \( \det(\lambda I - A) \) is always \( -\operatorname{tr}(A) = -([[a11]]+[[a22]]+[[a33]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the minus sign; the coefficient is \( -\operatorname{tr}(A) \), not \( \operatorname{tr}(A) \).
This multiplies the diagonal entries (related to the determinant), not their sum.
This sums off-diagonal entries instead of the diagonal (trace) entries.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by two from the correct coefficient.

stem: For \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] & [[i]] \end{pmatrix} \), the characteristic polynomial \( p(\lambda) = \det(\lambda I - A) \) has constant term \( p(0) = (-1)^3\det(A) = -\det(A) \). What is \( \det(A) \)?
distractor_rationales: This uses all plus signs instead of the alternating \( +,-,+ \) cofactor pattern.
This is \( -\det(A) \) (i.e. \( p(0) \) itself), the negative of what was asked.
This is the trace of \( A \), not its determinant.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by two from the correct value.

stem: A \( 3\times3 \) matrix \( A \) has characteristic polynomial \( p(\lambda) = (\lambda - ([[e1]]))(\lambda - ([[e2]]))(\lambda - ([[e3]])) \). What is the coefficient of \( \lambda \) in \( p(\lambda) \)?
solution: Expanding, \( p(\lambda) = \lambda^3 - (e_1+e_2+e_3)\lambda^2 + (e_1e_2+e_1e_3+e_2e_3)\lambda - e_1e_2e_3 \), so the coefficient of \( \lambda \) is the sum of pairwise products of the eigenvalues: \( [[e1]]\cdot[[e2]] + [[e1]]\cdot[[e3]] + [[e2]]\cdot[[e3]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the sum of the eigenvalues (negative of the \( \lambda^2 \) coefficient), not the \( \lambda \) coefficient.
This is the product of all three eigenvalues (negative of the constant term), not the \( \lambda \) coefficient.
This is the constant term of \( p(\lambda) \), not the \( \lambda \) coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by one from the correct coefficient.
This is off by two from the correct coefficient.
